coredata.dc:title: Detection of Fe3+ and Hg2+ ions through photoluminescence quenching of carbon dots derived from urea and bitter tea oil residue
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Chechia
Wang, Ke-Hsuan
Chen, Yu-Yu
Maniwa, Motoki
Andrew Lin, Kun-Yi
Kawai, Takeshi
Chen, Wei
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we prepared nitrogen-doped carbon dots (xNCDs) using hydrothermally-treated bitter tea oil residue with urea for the detection of metal ions by monitoring the photoluminescence quenching. The quantum yields of the xNCDs increased from approximately 3.85% (CDs) to 5.5% (3NCDs) and 7.2% (1NCDs), revealing that nitrogen doping effectively increases the fluorescence emission. The increased emission of the xNCDs can be attributed to radiative recombination resulting from the π-π* transition of the C=C or the n-π* transition between the C=O or N=O of sp3 units. Moreover, the CDs have abundant surface-attached phenolic and hydroxyl groups that coordinate with Fe3+ ions and quench the fluorescence. Conversely, Hg2+ ions preferentially adsorb on nitrogen-containing groups, such as amide-carbonyl groups (O=C-NH2) and pyridinic and pyrrolic functionalities, on the surface of the NCDs owing to their strong affinity, quenching the substantial photoluminescence emissions. Our results suggest that bitter tea oil residue-derived carbon dots can be used to selectively detect metal ions, such as Fe3+ and Hg2+, by doping with nitrogen using urea as a nitrogen precursor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bitter tea residue
Nitrogen doping
Carbon dots
Metal detection
Photoluminescence

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation of xNCDs (x = 1 and 3, which indicates the weight of urea used in the preparation) from bitter tea residue (BP) and urea.
TEM images of (a) CDs, (b) 1NCDs, and (c) 3NCDs, and the corresponding (d) XRD patterns. Insets (a) and (c) show TEM images.
(a) FTIR and (b) Raman spectra of CDs, 1NCDs, and 3NCDs.
XPS spectra of CDs, 1NCDs, and 3NCDs: (a) survey, and high resolution (b) C 1s, (c) O 1s, and (d) N 1s spectra.

(a) UV–vis and (b) PL spectra of CDs and xNCDs (x = 1, 2, and 3) samples obtained at excitation wavelength of 300 nm. Inset (a) shows photos of these samples in the viewing cabinet under light irradiation at 365 nm.
PL spectra of (a) CD and (b) 1NCD samples with addition of FeCl3 aqueous solution at different concentrations (0, 5, 8, 10, 25, 50, 75, and 100 ppm). Excitation wavelength = 300 nm.
PL spectra of (a) CD and (b) 1NCD samples with addition of Hg2NO3 aqueous solution at different concentrations (0, 5, 8, 10, 25, 50, 75, and 100 ppm). Excitation wavelength = 300 nm.
Stern-Volmer plot of CDs and 1NCDs containing Fe3+ or Hg2+ ions at different concentrations. Solid and hollow symbols refer to Fe3 + or Hg2+ ions, respectively.
UV–Vis for Fe3+ and Hg2+-containing CDs and 1NCDs solutions and the corresponding photos: (a) CDs-Fe3+, (b) 1NCDs-Fe3+, (c) CDs-Hg2+, and (d) 1NCDs-Hg2+.
Atomic ratio of CDs and xNCDs samples (x = 1 and 3) obtained by XPS.

head.ce:title: Detection of Fe3+ and Hg2+ ions through photoluminescence quenching of carbon dots derived from urea and bitter tea oil residue

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Taiwan University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we prepared nitrogen-doped carbon dots (xNCDs) using hydrothermally-treated bitter tea oil residue with urea for the detection of metal ions by monitoring the photoluminescence quenching. The quantum yields of the xNCDs increased from approximately 3.85% (CDs) to 5.5% (3NCDs) and 7.2% (1NCDs), revealing that nitrogen doping effectively increases the fluorescence emission. The increased emission of the xNCDs can be attributed to radiative recombination resulting from the π-π* transition of the C=C or the n-π* transition between the C=O or N=O of sp3 units. Moreover, the CDs have abundant surface-attached phenolic and hydroxyl groups that coordinate with Fe3+ ions and quench the fluorescence. Conversely, Hg2+ ions preferentially adsorb on nitrogen-containing groups, such as amide-carbonyl groups (O=C-NH2) and pyridinic and pyrrolic functionalities, on the surface of the NCDs owing to their strong affinity, quenching the substantial photoluminescence emissions. Our results suggest that bitter tea oil residue-derived carbon dots can be used to selectively detect metal ions, such as Fe3+ and Hg2+, by doping with nitrogen using urea as a nitrogen precursor.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal ion detection has attracted significant attention owing to increasing pollution from pulp, textile, pharmaceuticals, dyes, and pigments, semiconducting and electronic materials that significantly threaten our eco-system and result in deleterious effects on human health [1–3]. Among numerous strategies, the selective detection of heavy metals by monitoring the photoluminescence (PL) quenching of carbon-based dots (CDs) is the most attractive because it is rapid and boasts a wide range and low limit of detection. The PL of CDs originates from the photo-induced charge transfer and is easily observed [4]. Therefore, the PL is quenched significantly when specific metal ions are added, as they form chemical or physical bonds with the CDs [5,6]. To maintain consistent PL intensity, the surface morphology and structural, optical, and chemical properties of the CDs should be carefully controlled during preparation and when selecting raw materials.

CDs, with their fascinating optical, electronic, and thermal conductive properties, are widely reported and investigated because of their promise and potential as electrocatalysts, photocatalysts, sensitizers, catalyst supports, and materials for bio-imaging, labeling, and sensing [7,8]. Generally, citric acid, urea, and their derivatives are considered suitable nitrogen sources to prepare nitrogen-doped CDs and are known to exhibit better PL emission than the alternative [9,10]. Zhang et al. synthesized nitrogen-doped, carbon-rich CDs from ammonium citrate with bright blue luminescence and high stability [11]. Xiong reported that N- and S-codoped CDs with prominent C=N and C-N bonds could significantly enhance the fluorescence efficiency for multicolor cell imaging [12]. Moreover, the carbonization of nitrogen and carbon-rich precursors including 3‐(3,4‐dihydroxy phenyl)‐L‐alanine (L‐DOPA), L‐histidine, and L‐arginine was investigated by Yin et al., revealing that the doped nitrogen and low oxidation level in N-CDs could improve the luminescence emission [13]. However, due to the limitations of natural resources, a green, simple, cost-effective, and renewable strategy for the development of CDs is necessary to meet the demands of the circular economy and promote sustainability [14–19]. Bitter tea oil residue is an inexpensive and abundant agricultural waste, which should be reused to reduce its environmental impact. Urea is an amine-rich organic substance that has been widely used as a nitrogen precursor because it is a cheaper and safer N source compared to the common NH3 gas [20–22]. The type of precursors significantly affects the properties of the CDs. Also, nitrogen content, surface functional groups, and the localized sp2 domain within the carbon matrix significantly influence the charge recombination, and hence the PL, optical properties, and metal sensing performance [23–25]. The study of the synergistic effects of agriculture waste and urea as precursors is extremely important to promote a better understanding of the origin of the PL and metal sensing for CDs. However, to the best of our knowledge, no reports have been made on the synthesis of CDs from bitter oil residues and urea, or on the role of chemical, physical, and optical properties of CDs in heavy metal detection.

Herein, a bottom-up approach to prepare CDs for heavy metal detection using bitter oil residues, a type of agriculture waste, and nitrogen-rich urea is presented. Correlations between the nitrogen contents, the crystalline structure of the sp2/sp3 domain, and the optical properties were examined. CDs with appropriate nitrogen concentrations exhibited a PL quantum yield of 7.2% because of their C=N and C-N bonds and surface-attached –NH2 groups. Consequently, Fe3+ and Hg2+ ions could be detected by inhibiting the PL of CDs according to the Stern-Volmer equation. In this study, our results demonstrated that CDs derived from bitter oil residue and urea are cost-effective, easy to prepare, and effective for heavy metal detection.

Bitter tea oil residue was washed with aqueous methanol solution and deionized (DI) water three times, ground thoroughly, and then introduced to a tubular furnace for heat treatment at 700 °C for 6 h (ramp rate is set at 5 °C min−1) in an air atmosphere. The products were collected, ground, washed with DI water, dried at 60 °C, and named BP. To prepare N-doped CDs, BP was then suspended in solutions containing different weights of urea (1 or 3 g, Showa, Japan > 99%) in DI water and sealed in an autoclave for hydrothermal treatment at 160 °C for 12 h, and the experimental condition was similar to that in the previous reports [26–28]. Then the solution was subjected to centrifugation at 9000 rpm for 15 min, and the precipitate was removed to obtain a transparent suspension containing N-doped CDs (xNCDs, where x = 1 or 3 according to the weight of urea). The schematic illustration of the synthesis process is shown in Fig. 1 .

A Fourier transform infrared spectroscope (FTIR; Tensor 27, Bruker, USA) and an X-ray photoelectron spectroscope equipped with an electron spectroscope were employed for elemental analysis (XPS-ESCA; VG Scientific ESCALAB 250, UK) using Al Kα radiation to determine the chemical environments and oxidation states of the samples. The binding energy was calibrated using the C 1s peak at 284.8 eV in XPS. The crystal structures of the samples were analyzed using the X-ray diffraction (XRD) technique via a powder X-ray diffractometer (Bruker D8, USA) using a Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15418 nm) at 2θ angles of 5–80° and a scan rate of 6° min−1. The surface morphology and appearance of the samples were investigated using a transmission electron microscope (TEM; Hitachi, H-7500, Japan). Raman spectra were recorded by a scanning monochromator (Jobin Yvon, with 1,200 lines mm−1 holographic grating, blazed for 630 nm) using a diode-pumped solid-state laser excited at 640 nm to examine the samples. Optical properties, including light absorption and PL analysis, were examined using a UV–Vis spectrometer (UV–Vis, Hitachi U-3900, Japan) and a Spex Fluorolog-3 spectrofluorometer (Horiba, Japan) equipped with a Xenon light (450 W) and monochromators. The luminescence of the samples was directly observed using a viewing cabinet equipped with a UV lamp with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. A standard, fluorescein, and a solvent, aqueous NaOH solution (0.1 M), were employed to determine the quantum yield of the xNCD aqueous solution based on the equation:[29]
Q
y
( x )
=
(
I x
/
I
st
)
(
η x
/
η
st
)
(
A x
/
A
st
)
Q
y
( s t )

where I refers to the integrated luminescent intensity, η indicates the refractive index, A is the absorbance obtained from the UV–Vis spectra, Qy is the quantum yield in which x corresponds to the measured compound, and st denotes the standard solution.

A fixed volume of xNCD aqueous solution was diluted to 1 mL using DI water, and its fluorescence intensity at 420 nm upon excitation with a wavelength of 300 nm was measured to obtain I0 . The PL spectra were recorded to determine the selectivity by individually adding Fe3+ (FeCl3) or Hg2+ (HgCl2). Specifically, these ions were detected at different concentrations (5, 8, 10, 25, 50, 75, and 100 ppm) of Fe3+ or Hg2+ solution by adding a fixed amount of xNCD sample and measuring the PL intensity of the solution.

The size, morphology, and appearance of the xNCDs are displayed in Fig. 2 . The xNCDs exhibited a particle size of approximately 50–80 nm with an almost spherical shape. Insets of Fig. 2a and c showed that the xNCDs were all uniformly distributed, but some large carbon sheets remained in the CDs as shown in the inset of Fig. 2a. These TEM observations suggest that xNCDs exhibited similar configurations and appearance when treated with different concentrations of urea. Fig. 2d shows the XRD patterns of these samples, revealing three peaks centered at approximately 12–13°, 23–24°, and 43°. The peak at 12–13° corresponds to the diffraction plane (001) of graphitic carbon and results from its sp3 layer spacing; whereas the peak at 23–24° represents the diffraction spacing of the (002) plane of graphite. Notably, these peaks appeared at slightly higher or lower diffraction angles compared to the graphite oxide in the references [30]. This was mostly due to the abundant bonded hydroxyls, carbonyls, epoxys, and amino groups that influence the lattice structure of these CDs and xNCDs [31]. Diffraction peaks of 1NCDs and 3NCDs did not move toward higher or lower diffraction angles compared to those of the CDs, and this result could be attributed to the relatively low content of nitrogen doping, insertion, or grafting of nitrogen-containing groups on their lattice units.

The chemical structures of the resulting CDs, 1NCDs, and 3NCDs were investigated using FTIR, and the results are shown in Fig. 3 a. Absorption peaks, C-H at 2925 (–CH3) and 2850 (–CH2) cm−1, C=O at 1681 cm−1, aromatic C=C at 1586 cm−1, C–O at 1254 cm−1, and C–O–C at 1026 cm−1 were observed in the spectrum of the CD sample. The peak at approximately 2350 cm−1 could be attributed to the vibration band of the C=O bond of the surface adsorbed CO2 [32]. Another peak at ca. 3412 cm−1 corresponding to O-H stretching was also found [12,33,34]. This observation suggests that CDs were covered by –COOH and –OH groups. After the hydrothermal treatment, these characteristic 1NCDs and 3NCDs peaks shifted slightly. Moreover, peaks from C-N stretching vibrations (1100–1300 cm−1) and C-N/C=N ring stretching vibrations (1400–1600 cm−1) were observed [35]. These results suggest that 1NCDs and 3NCDs contain pyridinic and pyrrolic functionalities. The samples were also investigated using Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 3b). Two signature bands were observed in the spectra for all samples, ca. 1590 cm−1 (G band), attributed to the sp2-hybridized carbon phase, and ca. 1335 cm−1 (D band), originating from the defects associated with the sp3-hybridized carbon phase. The D to G band intensity ratio (ID/IG) is an efficient indicator of the crystallinity of carbon-based materials [36]. The ID/IG ratio calculated for the CD sample was 1.42, which implies that the residue-derived CDs contained a lower crystalline structure than those reported in previous studies. While the ID/IG ratio of the CDs was 1.4, the 1NCD and 3NCD ratios dropped to 1.29 and 1.26, respectively. This may have occurred because the hydrothermal treatment could aid the formation of the sp2 carbon phase in 1NCDs and 3NCDs.

XPS was used to obtain direct information about the surface composition and chemical state. The XPS survey spectra of the CDs indicate that C (285 eV) and O (531 eV) were present in the CD sample, as well as a small quantity of N (400 eV) and Mg (1304 eV, Fig. 4 a). The small amount of Mg content could be due to the impurity present in the bitter tea oil residue, which is commonly seen in the soil or agricultural waste [37]. The Gaussian function was used to peak fit a high-resolution XPS spectrum. In the high-resolution C 1s spectra (Fig. 4b), three fitted peaks at 284.5, 286, and 287 eV were attributed to the C=C/C-C, C-O, and C=O groups of each sample [12,38,39]. The O 1s spectrum of the CDs revealed a broad peak around 532.1 eV, whereas those of 1NCDs and 3NCDs were shifted by 0.5 eV to higher energy (Fig. 4 c). These O 1s spectra can be fitted with C=O and C–OH chemical bonding at 531.5 and 532.7 eV. As noted in Table 1 , the ratio of the O to C was 0.229 for CDs and increased to 0.298 for 1NCDs and 0.357 for 3NCDs due to the chemical transformation after hydrothermal treatment. This supports the hypothesis that the hydrothermal treatment can aid in the formation of the sp2 carbon phase. Moreover, the intensity of the N 1s peak for the CDs was slightly lower than those for the 1NCDs and 3NCDs (Fig. 4d). The CDs exhibited a lower N percentage of 2.4% than those for the 1NCDs and 3NCDs (3.4% and 3.8%, respectively, see N/C ratio in Table 1), which confirms that N atoms were doped into the 1NCDs and 3NCDs.

Fig. 5 a shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of the CD and xNCD samples, revealing that xNCDs exhibit broad and intense light absorption between 200 and 300 nm. The peak at approximately 230 nm represents the π-π* transition in the aromatic units of the CDs,[7] whereas the peak at 290 nm is ascribed to the absorption from the n-π* transition of C=O or N=O in the sp3 hybrid system [6]. The peak at ca. 240–250 nm for the 1NCDs is broader than those of the 3NCDs and CDs and results from the trapping of excited-state energy of the surface states, which might have caused the strong fluorescence [40]. These surface states could be attributed to excess nitrogen doping and the lone electrons in the xNCD structure, which contributed to both radiative and non-radiative recombination. The PL spectra in Fig. 5b indicated that the 1NCDs exhibited strong fluorescence, and the 3NCDs and CDs exhibited fluorescence to a lesser extent at 300 nm. The inset of Fig. 5a shows photos of the CD and xNCD solutions irradiated at a wavelength of 365 nm, demonstrating their blue emissive nature. The substantial emission of the NCDs could have originated from the radiative recombination of emissive traps of the π-π* transition of C=C or the n-π* transition between the C=O or N=O sp3 units. However, the mechanism is too complex to be clarified at this stage [7,41]. The quantum yields (QYs) of these CDs and xNCDs were measured to be approximately 3.85% (CDs), 7.2% (1NCDs), and 5.5% (3NCDs), suggesting that N-doping effectively improves the QYs.

We utilized the CDs and 1NCDs as probes to detect the PL emission and quenching with the addition of different concentrations of Fe3+ ions (5, 8, 10, 25, 50, 75, and 100 ppm), as shown in Fig. 6 . The results demonstrate that the PL of CDs and 1NCDs was quenched more significantly when larger quantities of Fe3+ were added. Previous studies have suggested that the surface hydroxyl and phenolic or amino groups could selectively coordinate with Fe3+, transferring the excited electrons to the half-filled 3d orbitals of Fe3+ ions and inducing the d-d transitions of the metal ion for PL quenching [42].

In addition, another toxic and bioaccumulated metal ion, mercury (Hg2+), was investigated for PL quenching, with different concentrations of Hg2+ added to the CD and 1NCD solutions (Fig. 7 ). With the addition of Hg2+, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs and 1NCDs decreased significantly, indicating that both the CDs and 1NCDs are sensitive as probes to detect Hg2+ ions. Many researchers have demonstrated that Hg2+ has a strong affinity for nitrogen-containing groups, such as pyridinic and pyrrolic functionalities (demonstrated using FTIR spectroscopy and XPS) on the nitrogen-doped CDs, therefore the PL emission could be significantly inhibited because of the electron transfer from the excited states of NCD to the d-orbitals of Hg2+ ions [40,43]. Notably, the 1NCD PL was quenched remarkably (Fig. 7b), revealing that Hg2+ ions significantly affect the radiative emission sites of N-doped CDs.

Fig. 8 shows the dependence of F0 /F on the concentrations of Fe3+ or Hg2+ ions, where F and F0 are the fluorescence intensity of the samples with and without the addition of metal ions, respectively. The relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the ion concentration is linear and follows the Stern-Volmer equation: (F0 -F) = 1 + Ksv [Q], where Ksv and [Q] are the quenching rate coefficient and the concentration of the quenchers, respectively. Note that the linear curves exhibited a high regression coefficient of determination (R2 ) for a wide concentration range of 2–100 ppm. The CDs and 1NCDs have high Ksv values, which are derived from the slope of the linear Stern-Volmer plots, for Fe3+ and Hg2+ detection [9,44]. This means that the nitrogen doping of CDs plays an important role in selectively trapping Hg2+ ions for PL quenching. As mentioned above, the CDs had pronounced phenolic and hydroxyl groups on their surfaces or edges, enabling coordination with Fe3+ ions, which resulted in the transfer of electrons from excited states to the half-filled d-orbitals of Fe3+ ions and fluorescence quenching [45]. In contrast, Hg2+ ions preferentially adsorbed on nitrogen-containing groups, such as amide-carbonyl groups (O=C-NH2) and pyridinic and pyrrolic functionalities, due to their strong affinity for the N-sites of NCDs [44,46,47]. This resulted in enhancing the PL quenching of xNCDs. In addition to the detection of the metal cations, anion detection using xNCDs was rarely seen and exhibits an off–on phenomenon to detect cation–anion pairs [48,49]. Some researchers have concluded that carbon-based dots could be promising materials to probe different ionic compounds.

Fig. 9 shows the UV–vis spectra of metal-containing (Fe3+ or Hg2+) CD and 1NCD solutions. Notably, the absorption intensity was smaller and the curves for 1NCDs-Fe3+ were flatter (Fig. 9b) than those of CDs-Fe3+ (Fig. 9a). This was attributed to the fact that the CDs contained more oxygen-containing groups, such as phenolic and hydroxyl groups, that absorbed light for electron transfer from the n-π* states, which preserved the light absorption at approximately 200–230 nm, while the 1NCDs contained fewer oxygen-containing groups and hence, there was less light absorption in this region. Moreover, the light absorption of CDs and 1NCDs in the range of 240–260 nm decreased significantly with Hg2+ adsorption, demonstrating that Hg2+ ions preferentially attach to the N-containing surface states and N-containing functional groups, similar to in the previous study [44]. However, the mechanism behind the red-shift observed in the CD- and NCD-metal systems at high metal concentrations is still unclear. Although, one possibility is that the adsorbed metal ions create a discrete level that receives electrons and enhances light absorption [43].

We successfully doped nitrogen into CDs prepared from bitter tea oil residue and urea using a hydrothermal treatment. The fluorescence emission of the NCDs originated from the synergistic electron transition from the π-π* of C=C and n-π* between the C=O or N=O sp3 units. Thus, the quantum yield of the 1NCDs increased by 7.2% compared to that of non-doped CDs (3.85%). The NCDs can be used to selectively detect metal ions, such as Fe3+ and Hg2+, by monitoring the PL quenching. Before nitrogen doping, the CDs exhibited pronounced phenolic and hydroxylic groups, whereas the nitrogen-doped xNCDs had abundant amide-carbonyl (O=C-NH2), pyridinic, and pyrrolic groups. The Fe3+ and Hg2+ ions preferentially coordinated to the carbon- and nitrogen-containing groups of CDs and NCDs, respectively, to quench the PL. We demonstrated that nitrogen-containing bitter tea oil residue-derived CDs can be used as facile, green, and effective materials for the selective detection of Fe3+ or Hg2+ ions.
The data and materials underlying this article are available on reasonable request to the corresponding author.

Chechia Hu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Ke-Hsuan Wang: Investigation. Yu-Yu Chen: Formal analysis, Investigation. Motoki Maniwa: Investigation. Kun-Yi Andrew Lin: Writing – review & editing. Takeshi Kawai: Supervision. Wei Chen: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan (MOST 107-2221-E-011-156-MY3, 108-2221-E-011-165-MY3, 110-2221-E-011-012-MY3).
